g.text: Trace: "White player moves Pawn e2->e4. Validate: is it Pawn's valid move? Path clear? No friendly piece at destination? Execute. Switch turn."
"Game is the orchestrator. Owns two players,
a board, current turn, status, and move history."
5. If valid: move piece, capture if needed
captures: who moved, from where, to
where, which piece, what was captured."
movesHistory stores all moves for replay."
holds a Piece (or nullptr if empty)
Polymorphism is the core design here.
Each concrete piece overrides it with
Bishop. Iterates from start to end,
checks no piece blocks the path.
which piece type it's dealing with."
pawn->isValidMove(): is it forward 2? First move? Path clear?
Destination empty? YES -> move pawn. Black plays e7->e5.
White plays Bishop f1->c4. Game calls bishop->isValidMove():
is it diagonal? rowDiff==colDiff? Path clear? YES. Eventually
a King is captured -> checkmate -> game over."
The entire design is built on one concept: polymorphism.
Game never checks 'if piece is King then...' — it just calls
piece->isValidMove() and each piece type handles its own rules."
Game: playMove(), validate, execute, switch turn
Board 8x8 Cells, Cell holds Piece at Position
makeMove: validate ownership -> polymorphic move check -> execute
Movement rules: King(1), Queen(any), Rook(straight), Bishop(diag), Knight(L), Pawn(fwd)
: Game calls piece->isValidMove() polymorphically. Never checks piece type. OCP + LSP.
